title: NASA
subtitle: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration, the United States federal agency responsible for the civilian space program, aeronautics research, and space science, covering Artemis, the James Webb Space Telescope, and the future of deep space exploration
content_html:
Overview

NASA (National Aeronautics and Space Administration) is the United States government agency responsible for the nation's civilian space program and for aeronautics and aerospace research. Founded on July 29, 1958, by President Dwight D. Eisenhower in response to the Soviet Sputnik launch, NASA has led or participated in every major milestone of the space age, from the first American in orbit to the Moon landings, the Hubble Space Telescope, the Mars rovers, and now the Artemis program returning humans to the lunar surface.

As of 2026, NASA operates with an annual budget of approximately $24 billion and employs roughly 18,000 civil servants across ten field centers, supplemented by tens of thousands of contractor personnel. The agency's priorities span human spaceflight, robotic planetary science, Earth observation, aeronautics, and the emerging commercial space economy through partnerships with SpaceX, Boeing, Blue Origin, and Sierra Nevada Corporation.

For ongoing NASA mission news, breaking space discoveries, and science reporting, follow OzoneDailyNews, OzoneNews's dedicated science and space news source, alongside the NASA hub here.

Artemis | Returning Humans to the Moon

The Artemis program is NASA's flagship human spaceflight initiative, with the stated goal of landing the first woman and the next man on the lunar surface and establishing a sustainable human presence on and around the Moon by the late 2020s. The program takes its name from the twin sister of Apollo in Greek mythology.

Artemis I, an uncrewed test flight of the Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and Orion crew capsule, launched successfully in November 2022 and completed a 25-day mission around the Moon. The mission validated SLS performance, Orion's heat shield at lunar return velocities, and the full integrated stack before human crew.

Artemis II, the first crewed Artemis mission, is scheduled to carry four astronauts on a free-return lunar flyby without landing. The crew includes Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen. Koch will be the first woman to fly to lunar distance. The mission represents the furthest humans have traveled from Earth since Apollo 17 in December 1972.

Artemis III, targeting the first crewed lunar surface landing since Apollo 17, is planned to use SpaceX's Starship Human Landing System (HLS) to descend from lunar orbit to the surface. SpaceX won the HLS contract in April 2021 over a competing Blue Origin bid, triggering a protest that delayed the program by nearly a year. The landing site will be near the lunar south pole, where permanently shadowed craters are believed to contain water ice that could be used for fuel and life support on future long-duration missions.

The Lunar Gateway, a small space station in lunar orbit that will serve as a staging point for Artemis surface missions, is under development with international partners including ESA, JAXA, and CSA. Gateway's first modules are scheduled for launch in the mid-2020s.

James Webb Space Telescope | Rewriting Cosmology

The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) launched on December 25, 2021, and began full science operations in July 2022 after a six-month commissioning period. Operating at the second Lagrange point (L2), 1.5 million kilometers from Earth, JWST is the largest and most powerful space telescope ever launched, with a 6.5-meter primary mirror composed of 18 gold-coated beryllium segments.

JWST observes the universe in the infrared spectrum, allowing it to see through dust clouds that block visible light and to detect the redshifted light of the earliest galaxies formed after the Big Bang. Its primary science goals include observing the formation of the first stars and galaxies, characterizing exoplanet atmospheres for signs of habitability, and studying star and planet formation within the Milky Way.

In May 2026, JWST published findings on the atmosphere of WASP-94Ab, a hot Jupiter exoplanet, detecting a predictable daily cloud cycle driven by the planet's extreme thermal gradient, the first such observation of a dynamic weather pattern on a world outside our solar system. Full coverage at JWST WASP-94Ab daily cloud cycle.

For the latest JWST discoveries and space science reporting, see OzoneDailyNews, our dedicated science and space vertical.

Mars Exploration | Rovers, Helicopters, and Sample Return

NASA has operated the most extensive robotic Mars exploration program of any space agency. The Curiosity rover, which landed in Gale Crater in August 2012, remains operational as of 2026, having driven over 30 kilometers and confirming that Mars once had liquid water capable of supporting microbial life.

The Perseverance rover landed in Jezero Crater in February 2021, the site of an ancient river delta identified as a high-priority astrobiology target. Perseverance carries the MOXIE instrument, which demonstrated oxygen production from Martian atmospheric carbon dioxide, a critical technology for future human missions. It also carried the Ingenuity helicopter, the first powered aircraft to fly on another planet. Ingenuity completed its final flight in January 2024 after suffering rotor blade damage.

The Mars Sample Return (MSR) mission, a joint NASA and ESA program designed to retrieve the rock and soil samples cached by Perseverance and return them to Earth, has faced significant cost and schedule pressure. In 2024, an independent review board found the original architecture was unaffordable at projected cost growth rates exceeding $10 billion, prompting NASA to solicit alternative architectures including commercial concepts from SpaceX and Blue Origin.

International Space Station | Final Years and Transition

The International Space Station (ISS) has been continuously inhabited since November 2000, making it the longest continuous human presence in space in history. The station, assembled across more than 40 flights over 13 years, spans the length of a football field and has been visited by over 270 people from 20 countries.

NASA has committed to operating the ISS through 2030, after which it will be deorbited in a controlled reentry over the ocean. The transition strategy involves handing low Earth orbit research functions to commercial successors. NASA has contracted with Axiom Space, Blue Origin, and Northrop Grumman under its Commercial Low Earth Orbit Destinations (CLD) program to develop private space stations to replace the ISS.

SpaceX's Crew Dragon and Boeing's Starliner are NASA's certified crew transportation providers to the ISS under the Commercial Crew Program. Starliner's first crewed test flight in 2024 experienced propulsion anomalies that extended the originally planned 8-day mission and raised questions about Boeing's path to operational certification.

Commercial Partnerships | SpaceX, Blue Origin, and the New Space Economy

NASA's relationship with the commercial space sector has been transformed over the past fifteen years by the Commercial Orbital Transportation Services and Commercial Crew programs, which pioneered the model of NASA as an anchor customer rather than a systems developer. This shift allowed SpaceX to develop Falcon 9, Dragon, and Starship on commercial capital while maintaining NASA as a key customer.

SpaceX holds the Starship HLS contract for Artemis lunar landing, Commercial Crew contracts for ISS crew rotation, and Commercial Resupply Services contracts for cargo delivery. The company's Starship vehicle, the largest rocket ever flown, completed its fourth integrated flight test in June 2024 and has continued rapid iterative development. SpaceX's role in the Artemis architecture makes it the single most important commercial partner in NASA's deep space program.

Blue Origin, founded by Jeff Bezos, lost the original HLS competition to SpaceX but was awarded a second HLS contract in 2023, giving NASA two lunar lander providers. Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket completed its first successful mission in January 2025 after years of schedule delays.

The commercial space economy extends well beyond NASA partnerships to include satellite communications (SpaceX Starlink, Amazon Kuiper), Earth observation, space tourism, and on-orbit servicing. For technology and science sector coverage, follow the OzoneNews Tech desk.

NASA in 2026 | Key Missions and Developments

NASA's 2026 priorities center on keeping the Artemis II crew mission on track, resolving the Mars Sample Return architecture, and maintaining the science output of JWST and the operational Mars rovers. Budget pressure from the federal government has introduced uncertainty into several science missions, including the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, scheduled for launch in 2027 to conduct wide-field surveys for dark energy and exoplanets.

The JWST science pipeline continues to produce transformative findings at a rate that has surprised even optimistic pre-launch projections. The telescope's sensitivity in near- and mid-infrared wavelengths has enabled atmospheric characterization of dozens of exoplanets and the detection of galaxies from less than 400 million years after the Big Bang, pushing the observational frontier of cosmology further than the Hubble Space Telescope could reach in 32 years of operation.

NASA's Earth science missions, including the PACE ocean color satellite launched in February 2024 and the NISAR synthetic aperture radar mission developed jointly with the Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO), continue to generate critical climate data on aerosols, sea surface temperatures, and land surface deformation.
